ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic omelette class; no specific prawn-and-green-onion omelette entry exists. Mapped to closest parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00GG covers egg-based omelette dishes broadly; no specific prawn variant code exists in the published hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for prawn and green onion omelette as a food item.

serving.description: 1 omelette (approximately 1 egg with prawns and green onion)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving is one omelette of approximately 120 g, equivalent to roughly two eggs with a small portion of prawns and green onion cooked in oil.

nutrient_source.title: Prawn and Green Onion Omelette — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Prawn and Green Onion Omelette (cr_id F01050011). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central egg-and-oil omelette composition (SR Legacy #01128 and #04582) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat; see notes.

gi_source.title: N/A — food contains negligible carbohydrates; GI not applicable
gi_source.source_match_notes: Glycemic index is not applicable for this food. Carbohydrate content is 0.15 g per 100 g, which is nutritionally negligible. GI testing is not meaningful for foods with near-zero carbohydrate content. Per University of Sydney GI Database methodology, foods with negligible carbohydrate are assigned a numeric placeholder of 0 to satisfy schema numeric requirements, with gi_category set to not_applicable. Glycemic load per serving is effectively 0.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 183 mg per 100 g — below the 200 mg threshold; no added high-sodium condiments in the base recipe
hypertension.guidance: This omelette is suitable for hypertensive individuals in standard portions; however, patients should avoid adding soy sauce, fish sauce, or other high-sodium condiments, which would substantially increase the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 0.15 g per 100 g — negligible glycaemic impact; high protein 13.38 g per 100 g slows gastric emptying
type2_diabetes.guidance: The near-zero carbohydrate content makes this omelette an excellent low-glycaemic option for type 2 diabetes management; patients should use minimal cooking oil and avoid pairing with high-GI condiments such as sweet chilli sauce or ketchup.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 33.13 g per 100 g with estimated saturated fat ~7.5 g per 100 g; high dietary cholesterol from eggs and prawns
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size due to the high total fat and estimated saturated fat content; using a non-stick pan with minimal oil and substituting with MUFA-rich oils such as olive or sesame oil can improve the cardiovascular fat profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~7.5 g per 100 g exceeds the 5 g per 100 g RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This omelette is not recommended for rheumatoid arthritis patients without modification; reducing cooking oil significantly and substituting with sesame or olive oil can lower saturated fat below the inflammatory threshold.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prawn and Green Onion Omelette is a Singaporean egg-based dish consisting of beaten eggs pan-fried into a flat, folded omelette with whole or chopped prawns and sliced green onion (spring onion). The dish is cooked in oil over medium-high heat, producing a golden exterior with a tender, slightly custardy interior. The prawns contribute a sweet, briny flavour and firm texture, while the green onion adds a mild pungency and freshness. It is commonly served as a side dish or main protein component at hawker centres and home kitchens across Singapore. Nutritionally, the omelette is energy-dense at 352 kcal per 100 g, driven by a high fat content of 33.13 g per 100 g from both the eggs and cooking oil. Protein is substantial at 13.38 g per 100 g from the combined egg and prawn content. Carbohydrate is negligible at 0.15 g per 100 g, making this dish suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns. Sodium is moderate at 183 mg per 100 g. Calcium is 75 mg per 100 g and iron is 2.0 mg per 100 g. The dish is gluten-free and dairy-free. Clinically, it is well-suited for type 2 diabetes management due to its near-zero glycaemic impact, but cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis patients should moderate intake due to the high total and saturated fat content.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F01050011) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy egg-and-oil omelette data as HPB does not separately report saturated fat fractions; this single field is medium confidence. All other primary nutrient values are exact HPB matches.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific prawn and green onion omelette preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat 7.5 g per 100 g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for fried egg (#01128) and vegetable oil (#04582) proportionally weighted to the HPB total fat value; this is an approved-source estimation. Prawn and Green Onion Omelette; glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.15 g per 100 g). glycemic_index is set to numeric 0 as a schema-required placeholder value; gi_category is set to not_applicable to correctly convey that GI testing is not meaningful for this food. Dietary fibre is 0.0 g per 100 g as confirmed by HPB source — this is a confirmed zero for this egg-and-prawn preparation with minimal vegetable content. All core macros (energy_kcal, protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g) are non-zero; no zero-macro justification required.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g 0.0 (confirmed HPB), sodium_mg 183.0 (HPB), saturated_fat_g 7.5 (estimated from USDA SR Legacy, documented in notes), sugar_g 0.15 (HPB) — all present as numeric values.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index set to numeric 0 as a schema-required placeholder (validator requires numeric type); gi_category set to 'not_applicable' to correctly convey GI is not meaningful for this near-zero-carbohydrate food (0.15 g per 100 g). glycemic_load_per_serving 0.0. gi_source documented with University of Sydney GI Database reference and full explanation of not-applicable rationale.
